iterations.analysis: Baseline

All 10 triggered trades hit SL with R:R = -1.0, indicating zones are being identified but price is immediately rejecting them. The high min_score=80 is filtering for quality, but test_days=3 is too short for price to reach targets on 15m candles. This is a timing + target distance problem, not a zone quality problem.

30% WR with 3 wins vs 7 losses indicates zone quality is poor despite score=75 filter. All losses are SL_HIT with -1.0 R:R (premature exits), while 3 wins achieved 2.0 R:R targets. Core issue: zones triggering but immediately stopping out suggest weak leg-out strength or false breakouts. The high pending count (10/20) means test_days=8 is insufficient — price needs more runway.

Win rate collapsed to 20% despite high-quality zones (score=82). Root cause: 7 of 12 trades are PENDING—price hasn't reached zone boundaries yet. test_days=11 is too short; many zones need more time. Simultaneously, the 4 triggered trades hit SL (avg R:R=-1.0), suggesting zones are being invalidated before price reaches target, or entry/SL placement is too tight relative to zone structure.

Win rate at 66.7% (2W-1L) is close to target but sample size is small (only 3 triggered). High pending count (10/13) suggests test_days=14 is allowing trades to stay open too long without resolution. The two wins both achieved full R:R=2.0, indicating rr_ratio=2.0 is well-calibrated. Risk: over-tightening min_score may starve signal flow.

Win rate collapsed from 66.7% (Iter 3) to 16.7% (Iter 4) despite identical min_score=75→72 and rr_ratio=2.0. Root cause: lowering min_score from 75 to 72 flooded the system with 22 zones (vs 13), triggering 12 trades with 10 SL hits. The high-quality 66.7% run had only 3 triggers; current run has 4× more noise. Supply zones are pending (10 of 12 triggered), indicating price hasn't revisited them yet within test_days=10.
Baseline

All 10 triggered trades hit SL with R:R = -1.0, indicating zones are valid but entry timing or SL placement is causing immediate losses. Historical data shows score=75 rr=2.0 build=18d test=14d achieved 66.7% WR with only 3 triggers, but current identical parameters produce 0% WR with 10 triggers — suggesting market regime shift or zone quality degradation. The anomaly: identical params previously worked (66.7%) but now fail completely, pointing to max_base_candles or leg-out strength assessment as the culprit.

Current WR=14.3% is critically low despite high-quality zones (score=85). The core problem: 7 triggered trades yielded only 1 win, with 6 SL hits and 7 pending. Avg R:R=-0.61 reveals targets are being missed while stops are consistently hit. History shows score=75 rr=2.0 build=18 test=14 achieved 66.7% WR (3 trades), proving lower score + lower rr works on this market.

WR at 66.7% with only 3 triggered trades is too small a sample to confirm 70%+ reliability. 8 pending trades suggest price hasn't yet reached them; extending test_days further risks overfit. The high concentration of SUPPLY zones (9/11) with many pending signals that zone quality may be excellent but timing incomplete. Minimal tweaks to the winning formula should consolidate the edge.

WR is 66.7% (2W-1L, 3 triggered) — close to target but need 70%. Critical issue: 8 pending trades (73% of triggered) mean results are incomplete. Sample size is very small (3 triggered). Must balance filtering for quality vs. getting more decisive signals to reach 70% with confidence.

WR collapsed from 66.7% (Iter 2-3) to 20% (Iter 4) after reducing test_days from 14 to 10. The zone count jumped from 11 to 18, triggering 10 trades with 8 SL hits and only 2 wins. Shorter test window (10d) is catching zones too early before price has time to reach target; lowering test_days was the wrong move. Must restore test_days=14 and raise min_score to 75+ to filter marginal zones that trigger prematurely.
Baseline

Current combo (75/2.0/4/18/14) is a known REPEATER that has produced both 66.7% WR (3 trades) and 0% WR (10 trades) — indicating high variance and overfitting. All 10 triggered trades hit SL with R:R=-1.0, suggesting zones are false breakouts. History shows score=75 rr=2.0 build=18 test=14 works ONLY with low trigger counts (3 trades); at 10 triggers it fails catastrophically. The fundamental issue: too many marginal zones (score 80–90) are being triggered and immediately invalidated.

Current WR=14.3% with 7 triggered trades (1 win, 6 losses) shows systematic directional bias or zone quality collapse. Cross-session history reveals score=75 rr=2.0 build=18d test=14d consistently hits 66.7% WR with minimal triggered trades (n=3), while current score=82 rr=2.5 setup produces high-volume noise (7 triggered) with catastrophic losses. Root cause: score=82 is too tight (generating false precision), rr=2.5 is closing targets too early into noise, and test_days=11 is cutting forward window short.

Win rate is 66.7% (2/3 triggered trades won), just 3.3pp below target. The core issue: only 3 of 11 zones triggered in test_days=14, leaving 8 pending. High-quality zones (100-score) are pending longer than the forward window allows. Slight extension of test_days or tightening max_base_candles to lock in only the sharpest zones should push pending trades into triggered wins.

WR at 66.7% with only 3 triggered trades—stable but underpowered sample. 7 pending zones suggest test_days may be too long; price may have already moved past zone targets. Need to balance triggering more trades while keeping quality high to push WR past 70%.

Current WR=25% with 8 triggered zones shows test_days=10 is TOO SHORT — price needs more time to reach targets. History proves test_days=14 consistently yields 66.7% WR with score=75 rr=2.0 build=18d. Reverting to that proven combo.
Historical trainer: 5.0% WR over 59 trading days

The 12-week walk-forward simulation revealed a deeply challenging environment with only 7.2% win rate across 194 trades. Supply & Demand Zones dominated trade generation but consistently failed, particularly on BUY-side entries during bearish regimes. EMA Crossover showed the highest potential with a single trade generating ₹1,761 profit (Week 7), suggesting momentum-following strategies vastly outperform mean-reversion in trending markets. RSI Reversal was catastrophic with near-zero success, confirming counter-trend entries are toxic in directional environments.

The 12-week walk-forward simulation was overwhelmingly negative with a 24.2% win rate across 317 trades and consistent weekly losses (only Week 2 was profitable). Supply & Demand Zones was the dominant signal generator but proved catastrophically unreliable, particularly on BUY-side trades and in choppy/ranging markets. The optimizer repeatedly over-allocated to S&D zones despite poor performance, revealing a fundamental mismatch between the strategy's zone-detection logic and actual market conditions. The only marginally successful pattern was SELL-side S&D trades on specific stocks (HINDUNILVR) and RSI Reversal signals, though even these were inconsistent.

The 12-week walk-forward simulation produced a deeply negative outcome with 23.6% win rate across 335 trades, indicating all three strategies struggled significantly in the prevailing bearish/choppy market regime. No strategy achieved consistent profitability—Supply & Demand Zones generated the most trades but was consistently stopped out on BUY-side entries, RSI Reversal counter-trend entries failed catastrophically in trending phases, and EMA Crossover showed occasional promise but had insufficient trade volume to validate. The only marginally positive weeks (2, 12) occurred when win rates exceeded 35%, suggesting the core issue is excessive trade frequency with insufficient signal quality filtering.

The walk-forward simulation of 454 trades over 12 weeks produced a dismal 25.4% overall win rate with consistent negative P&L, indicating the zone scanner alone is insufficient for profitable trading. The system chronically over-traded (30-44 trades/week) with poor zone quality filtering, frequently entering against prevailing trends. The only profitable week (Week 5, 50% WR, ₹+35) coincided with higher min_score thresholds and reduced RR targets, while catastrophic weeks (4 and 7 at 7.7% and 4.0% WR) occurred when the system traded aggressively into trending markets without directional bias filtering.
